title: Lattice fermion formulation via Physics-Informed Neural Networks: Ginsparg-Wilson relation and Overlap fermions
summary: We propose a novel, machine-learning-based framework for constructing lattice fermions using Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs). Our approach treats the formulation of the Dirac operator as an optimization problem guided by physical requirements, such as symmetries, locality and doubler-decoupling conditions. We first demonstrate that, when trained to satisfy the Ginsparg-Wilson (GW) relation as a soft constraint, a neural network reproduces the overlap fermion operator to high numerical accuracy and learns an effective sign-function mapping without explicitly using a prescribed polynomial or rational approximation. Secondly, we extend the framework from operator construction to machine-assisted algebraic discovery. Within a generalized polynomial ansatz, the network autonomously drives higher-order terms to zero and recovers the standard Ginsparg-Wilson relation. Remarkably, by changing the initial search bias, the same framework also finds a distinct solution corresponding to a Fujikawa-type generalized GW relation.

AI.tldr: A new machine-learning-based framework using Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) is introduced for constructing lattice fermions, which can reproduce the overlap fermion operator and discover algebraic solutions to the Ginsparg-Wilson relation, including a novel Fujikawa-type generalized solution.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to develop a more efficient and flexible method for constructing lattice fermions that satisfies physical requirements like symmetries, locality, and doubler-decoupling conditions, while also enabling the discovery of new algebraic solutions to the Ginsparg-Wilson (GW) relation.
AI.method: The researchers use Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) to formulate the Dirac operator as an optimization problem. The PINN is trained to satisfy the GW relation, reproducing the overlap fermion operator and learning an effective sign-function mapping. The method is then extended to allow the network to autonomously find higher-order terms and recover known or discover new GW relations.
AI.result: The PINN successfully reproduces the overlap fermion operator with high accuracy and learns an implicit sign-function mapping. Additionally, it recovers the standard GW relation and discovers a new solution corresponding to a Fujikawa-type generalized GW relation, demonstrating the versatility of the approach in both operator construction and algebraic discovery.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework demonstrates the potential of machine learning in lattice field theory, providing a powerful tool for the construction of lattice fermions and the discovery of new theoretical insights, such as the generalized GW relation.

title: Diffusion model for SU(N) gauge theories
summary: Implicit score matching provides a computationally efficient approach for training diffusion models and generating high-quality samples from complex distributions. In this work, we develop a score-matching framework for SU(N) lattice gauge theories, which can be extended to other Lie groups. We apply the method to SU(3) gauge configurations with the Wilson gauge action in two and four dimensions and assess the quality of the generated samples by comparison with Hybrid Monte Carlo (HMC) simulations. We show that the diffusion models can be successfully trained and applied for sampling the Wilson gauge action. For large values of inverse coupling, accurate reverse-time integration requires predictor-corrector schemes, for which we introduce a corrector based on Hamiltonian molecular dynamics. While the corrector significantly improves sampling quality, it also increases the computational cost. We outline several strategies for improving sampling efficiency.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a score-matching framework for SU(N) lattice gauge theories, demonstrating its effectiveness in training diffusion models and generating high-quality samples, especially for SU(3) gauge configurations. It also presents a corrector based on Hamiltonian molecular dynamics to improve sampling quality, though this comes with increased computational cost.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to develop a computationally efficient method for training diffusion models and generating high-quality samples from complex distributions, specifically focusing on SU(N) lattice gauge theories. The aim is to provide an alternative to existing methods like Hybrid Monte Carlo (HMC) simulations, which can be computationally expensive.
AI.method: A score-matching framework is developed for SU(N) lattice gauge theories, particularly applied to SU(3) gauge configurations using the Wilson gauge action in two and four dimensions. To enhance the accuracy of reverse-time integration, especially at large inverse coupling values, a corrector scheme based on Hamiltonian molecular dynamics is introduced.
AI.result: The results show that the diffusion models can be effectively trained and used for sampling the Wilson gauge action. The use of a predictor-corrector scheme, including the newly introduced corrector, significantly improves the quality of generated samples. However, this improvement comes at the expense of increased computational costs.
AI.conclusion: The study successfully demonstrates the application of implicit score matching for training diffusion models in the context of SU(N) lattice gauge theories, providing a viable approach for generating high-quality samples. While the introduction of a corrector enhances sample quality, addressing the associated increase in computational cost remains a challenge, prompting the need for further research into more efficient sampling strategies.

title: The strange and flavor-singlet axial form factors of the nucleon from lattice QCD
comment: 14 pages, 8 figures, 5 tables; prepared for submission to PRD
summary: The singlet axial form factor of the nucleon provides essential input for a complete understanding of the nucleon axial structure. Together with the isovector and isoscalar octet channels, in the forward limit it forms the basis for a full flavor decomposition of the proton spin. In this work we present a lattice QCD determination of the singlet axial form factor $G^{u+d+s}_A(Q^2)$ and related strange contribution $G^{s}_A(Q^2)$ using a set of $N_f = 2 + 1$ CLS gauge ensembles with $O(a)$-improved Wilson fermions, with a full error budget for the extrapolation to the chiral, continuum and infinite-volume limits. Particular focus is placed on the treatment of the disconnected contributions, which constitute the crucial element for the extraction of the strange component. Together with determinations of the isovector and isoscalar octet axial form factors, this work provides a comprehensive lattice QCD determination of the nucleon axial structure across different flavor channels.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a lattice QCD determination of the singlet axial form factor and the strange quark contribution to it, providing a comprehensive understanding of the nucleon's axial structure across different flavor channels.
AI.motivation: To provide essential input for a complete understanding of the nucleon's axial structure, including a full flavor decomposition of the proton spin, by determining the singlet axial form factor and the strange quark contribution.
AI.method: The authors use a set of $N_f = 2 + 1$ CLS gauge ensembles with $O(a)$-improved Wilson fermions in lattice QCD. They focus on treating the disconnected contributions, which are key to extracting the strange quark component. The analysis includes a full error budget for extrapolation to the chiral, continuum, and infinite-volume limits.
AI.result: The study provides a determination of the singlet axial form factor $G^{u+d+s}_A(Q^2)$ and the strange contribution $G^{s}_A(Q^2)$. It also contributes to the knowledge of isovector and isoscalar octet axial form factors, offering a broad picture of the nucleon's axial structure across various flavor channels.
AI.conclusion: The work represents a significant step towards a full flavor decomposition of the proton spin and an improved understanding of the nucleon's axial structure, with detailed insights into both the singlet and strange quark components.

title: $F_K/F_π$ as a precision test of a new four flavor Domain Wall Fermion action
summary: We present a new set of lattice QCD ensembles with four flavors of smeared Möbius Domain Wall Fermions with good chiral symmetry and small fifth-dimensional extent. A modest amount of computing resources was sufficient to generate 30 publicly-available ensembles spanning five lattice spacings and a broad range of pion masses down to physical. To scrutinize our action we determine $F_{K^{\pm}}/F_{π^{\pm}} = 1.1962(34)$, a key quantity for precision CKM unitarity tests, heralding a future of inexpensive high-precision calculations of hadronic observables with chiral fermions.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new set of lattice QCD ensembles with four flavors of smeared Möbius Domain Wall Fermions, which are characterized by good chiral symmetry and a small fifth-dimensional extent. The researchers were able to generate 30 public ensembles covering five lattice spacings and a wide range of pion masses. They also determined the ratio F_K/F_π, which is important for CKM unitarity tests, indicating potential for future high-precision calculations.
AI.motivation: The motivation behind this work is to develop a more efficient and precise method for generating lattice QCD ensembles that can be used for high-precision calculations of hadronic observables. By using an action with improved chiral symmetry and a reduced fifth-dimensional extent, the authors aim to facilitate these calculations with less computational resources.
AI.method: The authors utilized a new set of lattice QCD ensembles with four flavors of smeared Möbius Domain Wall Fermions. These ensembles were generated using a modest amount of computing resources, resulting in 30 publicly available configurations that span five different lattice spacings and a broad spectrum of pion masses, including those at the physical point.
AI.result: The result is the successful generation of 30 lattice QCD ensembles with desirable properties, and the determination of the key quantity F_K/F_π = 1.1962(34), which is crucial for precision CKM unitarity tests. This outcome suggests that the new fermion action can be effectively used for accurate calculations of hadronic observables with chiral fermions.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion drawn from the study is that the new lattice QCD ensembles with Möbius Domain Wall Fermions provide a promising approach for performing high-precision calculations of hadronic observables while requiring only a moderate amount of computational power. The findings open up possibilities for future studies to benefit from the enhanced chiral symmetry and reduced costs associated with the new fermion action.
